attributes.stem:
This question is beyond the expected knowledge level of a graduating medical student.

A 58-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for follow-up of her bronchiectasis. She has experienced four exacerbations requiring antibiotics in the past six months, each presenting with increased sputum production, purulence and dyspnoea. She has a 15-year history of bronchiectasis diagnosed on high-resolution CT scan. She has no other significant medical history. She does not take any regular medications. She is a non-smoker and works as a librarian. She is up to date with her vaccinations.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Chest auscultation reveals coarse crackles at both lung bases. She performs regular chest physiotherapy and uses hypertonic saline nebulisers. Extensive investigations for underlying causes have been unremarkable. Sputum cultures during exacerbations have grown common respiratory organisms on different occasions, but never Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Spirometry shows FEV1 1.8 L (65% predicted) and FVC 2.4 L (70% predicted).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate addition to her management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient with bronchiectasis has frequent exacerbations despite optimal airway clearance techniques. In such cases, prophylactic antibiotics can reduce exacerbation frequency and improve quality of life. The choice of antibiotic depends on the organisms previously cultured and local resistance patterns.

Regular azithromycin is the most appropriate choice for this patient. Long-term macrolide therapy (typically azithromycin 250-500mg three times weekly) has been shown to reduce exacerbation frequency in bronchiectasis by approximately 60%. Its benefit extends beyond antimicrobial effects to include anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory properties. It is particularly suitable for patients with recurrent infections from common respiratory pathogens, as seen in this case.

Whole lung lavage is a specialised procedure used primarily for pulmonary alveolar proteinosis and occasionally for severe lipoid pneumonia. It has no role in the management of bronchiectasis and would be potentially harmful in this setting.

Regular ciprofloxacin would be inappropriate as this fluoroquinolone is specifically reserved for patients with Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonisation. This patient has never grown Pseudomonas, and using ciprofloxacin unnecessarily risks promoting resistance and adverse effects including tendinopathy.

Regular inhaled corticosteroids are not routinely recommended in bronchiectasis unless there is coexistent asthma or COPD. Studies have shown no benefit in reducing exacerbations, and there are concerns about increased pneumonia risk with long-term use.

Long-acting bronchodilators may provide symptomatic relief in some bronchiectasis patients, particularly those with coexistent airway obstruction. However, they do not reduce exacerbation frequency and would not address this patient's primary problem of recurrent infections.

Before starting azithromycin, baseline liver function tests and ECG (to check QTc interval) should be performed. Patients should be monitored for hearing loss and gastrointestinal side effects. The treatment is typically continued for at least six months before assessing response.
learningPoints: Long-term azithromycin reduces exacerbation frequency in bronchiectasis through anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects beyond its antimicrobial properties.
Prophylactic antibiotics in bronchiectasis should be considered after optimising airway clearance and treating underlying causes.
Regular ciprofloxacin is reserved for patients with Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonisation, not for those with recurrent infections from common organisms.
references: Chang AB, Bell SC, Byrnes CA, Dawkins P, Holland AE, Kennedy E, et al. Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand (TSANZ) position statement on chronic suppurative lung disease and bronchiectasis in children, adolescents and adults in Australia and New Zealand. Respirol Carlton Vic. 2023 Apr;28(4):339–49.
Hill A.T., Sullivan A.L., Chalmers J.D., De Soyza A., Stuart Elborn J., Andres Floto R., et al. British thoracic society guideline for bronchiectasis in adults. Thorax. 2019;74(Supplement 1):74.

attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree

attributes.name: Level III
attributes.description: Apply, analyze, evaluate, and create (implementing, organizing, and critiquing information)
